Description: This is a red apple. A fruit. Rich in dietary fiber, vitamin C, and various antioxidants. Apples are low in calories and contain no fat, sodium, or cholesterol.
This is a half open banana. A fruit. High in potassium, vitamin B6, vitamin C, and dietary fiber. Bananas are also a good source of energy due to their carbohydrate content.

This is yellow bell pepper. Bell peppers are botanically classified as fruits. This is because they develop from the flower of the plant and contain seeds. However, in culinary terms, bell peppers are commonly treated and referred to as vegetables due to their savory flavor and use in cooking. Rich in vitamin C, vitamin A, and dietary fiber. Bell peppers are also low in calories and contain various antioxidants.
It is a vegetable basket.

It's  a tomato. Tomatoes are also botanically classified as fruits because they develop from the flower of the tomato plant and contain seeds. However, like bell peppers, tomatoes are commonly treated as vegetables in culinary contexts due to their savory flavor and use in a wide variety of dishes.A  High in vitamin C, potassium, folate, and vitamin K. Tomatoes also contain the antioxidant lycopene, which has been linked to many health benefits.
It's a potato. A vegetable. Rich in carbohydrates, vitamin C, vitamin B6, and potassium. Potatoes are also a good source of dietary fiber, especially when eaten with the skin.

It's a pumpkin. Pumpkins are botanically classified as fruits because they develop from the flowering part of the pumpkin vine and contain seeds. However, in culinary terms, pumpkins are often treated as vegetables due to their versatile use in cooking, particularly in savory dishes such as soups, stews, and pies. So, while they are fruits from a botanical perspective, they are typically referred to and used as vegetables in culinary contexts. High in vitamin A, vitamin C, potassium, and dietary fiber. Pumpkins are also low in calories and contain antioxidants like beta-carotene.
